# Lab/LCH/OKLab/OKLCH

<FeatureLinks mdn="https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/search?q=Lab%2FLCH%2FOKLab%2FOKLCH%20CSS" caniuse="https://caniuse.com/?search=Lab%2FLCH%2FOKLab%2FOKLCH" status="Color 4" label="Reference links" />

## Plain-language explanation

Use perceptually uniform color spaces for design systems and gradients.

## Representative syntax

```css
oklch(70% 0.16 250)
```

## Demo

<CssDemo title="Lab/LCH/OKLab/OKLCH" css-b64="" code-b64="" html-b64="" caption="The important part is the relationship between colors: one semantic color generates a maintainable set of states." lang="en-US" badge="Practical demo" />

## When to use it

<div class="usage-grid">
<section>
  <h3>Best fit</h3>
  <p>Use Lab/LCH/OKLab/OKLCH in theme and state systems when you need to build color variations from semantic inputs instead of hand-tuning every state. It usually gives the stylesheet a clearer job than extra classes, wrappers, or script.</p>
</section>
<section>
  <h3>Example</h3>
  <p>A warning banner can derive its background, border, and text color from one token and still adapt in dark mode.</p>
</section>
<section>
  <h3>Watch out</h3>
  <p>Perceptual spaces help with ramps, but they do not replace contrast checks against real text sizes and backgrounds.</p>
</section>
</div>

## Source metadata

- Category: Color, Theming, and High Dynamic Range
- Maturity / source: Color 4
- Representative syntax: `oklch(70% 0.16 250)`
- Position in category: 3 / 15
